questions.q: Which interval is considered a perfect fifth in semitones?
questions.explanation: A perfect fifth spans 7 semitones between two notes. Five semitones would be a perfect fourth, not a fifth. Twelve semitones is an octave.

questions.q: In the C major scale, what are the seven notes in ascending order?
options: C D E F G A B
C D E♭ F G A B♭
C D F G A B C♯
C D E F♯ G A B
questions.explanation: The C major scale contains no sharps or flats. The sequence C-D-E-F-G-A-B is correct. Other options introduce accidentals that don't belong in C major.

questions.q: What is the relative minor key of G major?
questions.explanation: E minor is the relative minor of G major; they share the same key signature (one sharp). Relative minor keys are always three semitones below their relative major.

questions.q: A major triad built on C consists of which notes?
questions.explanation: A C major triad contains C (root), E (major third), and G (perfect fifth). C-E♭-G would be C minor; C-D-G omits the third; C-E-G♯ is incorrect spelling.

questions.q: What does a time signature of 6/8 indicate?
options: Six beats per measure, eighth note gets one beat
Six eighth notes per measure, quarter note gets one beat
Six quarter notes per measure, sixteenth note gets one beat
Six beats per measure, quarter note gets one beat
questions.explanation: In 6/8, the upper number is 6 beats and the lower 8 means the eighth note receives the beat. This is a compound meter with two beats of three eighth notes each.

questions.q: Which note is enharmonically equivalent to B♯?
questions.explanation: B♯ and C are enharmonically equivalent—they sound identical but have different letter names. This is important in music theory for proper voice leading and key signatures.

questions.q: What interval is formed between C and G♭?
questions.explanation: C to G is a perfect fifth (7 semitones); C to G♭ is 6 semitones, making it a diminished fifth. A diminished fifth and augmented fourth are enharmonically equivalent.

questions.q: In musical notation, what does a double sharp (𝄪) do to a note?
questions.explanation: A double sharp raises a note by two semitones. It's used in theoretical contexts to maintain proper letter names within a key or scale context.

questions.q: Which chord quality is created with a root, minor third, and perfect fifth?
questions.explanation: A minor chord contains a root, minor third (3 semitones up), and perfect fifth (7 semitones up). A major chord uses a major third instead.

questions.q: What is the interval name for the distance of 2 semitones?
questions.explanation: Two semitones equal a minor second (half step). A major second would be 2 whole steps (4 semitones). This is the smallest interval in Western music.
